DP-PostMerge: Free Compression via Post-hoc Merging
for Multi-Vector Visual Document Retrieval

Author 1  ·  Author 2  ·  Author 3
Institution Name
Conference
Logo
Problem, Motivation & Method
Problem & Motivation

Task — Visual Document Retrieval (VDR). With recent VLM breakthroughs, we can now retrieve documents by rich visual cues (tables, charts, layout, figures) — not just OCR text. SOTA models like ColPali / ColQwen encode each page as ~700 patch vectors.

Scoring: MaxSim late interaction. For each query token, find its best-matching patch on the page; sum those similarities:  s(q, d) = Σi maxj qi⊤dj. ⇒ retrieval quality depends on having a few sharp, distinctive patches that each query token can latch onto.

Problem. 700 vectors × millions of pages → storage bottleneck. A 1 000-page PDF alone ≈ 500 MB of embeddings — impractical considering a company has billions of documents.

Two research camps disagree on how to compress:

🔵 Merge camp — Light-ColPali (Ma et al. 2025): "Pruning is inherently unsuitable."
They tested throwing patches away at a fixed ratio. Past 50% compression, performance collapses, even random deletion beats attention-based rules.
Why: different queries need different patches, so dropping offline is always a gamble.
Their answer: don't throw patches away — merge similar ones (clustering, pooling).
🟠 Prune camp — DocPruner (Yan et al. 2025): "You pruned wrong."
Counter: all 3 strategies Light-ColPali tested (random, score-based, attention-based) are fixed-ratio — the weakest kind of pruning.
Fix: replace fixed ratio with a per-document adaptive threshold. Info-dense pages retain more; sparse pages retain less → 50–60% near-lossless compression.
On merging: averaging patches into centroids dilutes the sharp signals MaxSim relies on. In the low-to-moderate regime (~50–75% compression), DocPruner beats every merging baseline by ~3% nDCG@5.
💡 Our take — both are partially right.
  • DocPruner selects the right patches, but among the kept ones there are still near-duplicates.
  • Merging on raw patches is unsafe (blurs signal), but safe on an already-filtered, high-signal set.

Combine them: prune first to drop the noise, then merge the remaining duplicates. Free extra compression, no retrieval loss.

DP-PostMerge: Method
Code
Main Finding — ViDoRe-V2 (4 datasets, ColQwen2.5)
Overall Performance Comparison
nDCG@5 vs compression ratio, averaged across 4 ViDoRe-V2 datasets. DP-PostMerge (red stars) dominates the full compression spectrum, matching or exceeding DocPruner at every operating point while all prior baselines (sem-cluster, 1d/2d-pooling, attention-plus-similarity) are strictly below.
DP-PostMerge Pareto-dominates all baselines from 50% to 80% compression
Qualitative: What does DP-PostMerge actually merge?
① Original → ② DocPruner → ③ +PostMerge on an ESG report page (720 → 236 → 212 patches). DocPruner (grey = dropped) removes 484 low-attention patches; +PostMerge then collapses the colored groups below (3 representative examples) into a single weighted-average vector each. ★ = anchor patch.

Story: DocPruner keeps salient content (photos, titles, paragraphs). PostMerge fuses the remaining near-duplicate patches into single vectors — free compression, no retrieval loss.

Statistical Validation

Paired Wilcoxon, one-sided (PM > DP), Holm-corrected. At matched compression (k values offset so DP & PM reach the same rate); 672 per-query nDCG@5 scores pooled across 4 ViDoRe-V2 datasets.

Compression DocPruner DP-PostMerge Δ 95% CI padj sig
~52%0.5860.585−0.001 [−0.010, +0.008]0.74n.s.
~63%0.5700.584+0.015 [+0.004, +0.026]0.05*
~71%0.5530.574+0.022 [+0.011, +0.032]<0.001***
~77%0.5400.554+0.014 [+0.004, +0.025]0.05*
~85%0.5010.536+0.035 [+0.020, +0.051]<0.001***

✓ Significant improvement at every compression ≥ 63%
✓ Largest gain (+0.035) at ~85% compression — precisely where DocPruner collapses.

Results — ViDoRe-V3 & Takeaways
Generalization: ViDoRe-V3 (7 datasets, θ=0.93)
nDCG@5 vs compression ratio (avg across 7 ViDoRe-V3 datasets). Same pattern holds on a larger, more diverse benchmark — no hyperparameter tuning needed.
Generalizes to ViDoRe-V3 (7 datasets) with no hyperparameter search
Method Properties
  • Zero training cost — no learned parameters, no optimization
  • Model-agnostic — works on any multi-vector retrieval model
  • Composable — applies on top of any pruning method
  • Single hyperparameter — cosine similarity threshold θ
  • Negligible runtime — pairwise cosine on already-pruned patches
Key Takeaways
  1. DP-PostMerge provides 3–10% free compression on top of DocPruner at every operating point, with negligible quality loss
  2. Importance ≠ redundancy — DocPruner's attention threshold is near-optimal for selection, but leaves exploitable redundancy among kept patches
  3. Generalizes without tuning — consistent gains on both ViDoRe-V2 (4 datasets) and ViDoRe-V3 (7 datasets)
  4. Practical recommendation: Always apply post-hoc merging after pruning — it's free compression with no downside
Experiment Setup
PropertyValue
ModelColQwen2.5 (vidore/colqwen2.5-v0.2)
BenchmarksViDoRe-V2 (4 datasets), ViDoRe-V3 (7 datasets)
MetricnDCG@5
BaselineDocPruner (6 k values)
Threshold θ0.93–0.95 (cosine similarity)
References:  DocPruner — Yan et al., 2025  |  ColPali — Faysse et al., 2024  |  ViDoRe-V2 — Macé et al., 2025  |  Model: ColQwen2.5 (vidore/colqwen2.5-v0.2)
